author: @bodo9387
text: This is brilliant, thank you! There's pushes in a lot of places to have more trees and forest for biodiversity and to sequester greenhouse gases, but achieving that can sometimes seem count to food production and be competition for land use, so serious harvesting and utilising of acorns for food, be it for animal feed or for humans, could really mean we could have both.. semi-natural biodiverse woodland as well as food. Growing perennial trees like oaks for acorns, as a carbohydrate rich food source, also benefits the climate twice by the growing trees sequestering carbon, whilst at the same time lessening the need to grow crops like wheat for carbohydrates which cause carbon to be released into the atmosphere when grown by the process of tilling the land. The carbohydrate rich acorns could also be used as a feed for insect agriculture, which can then also be used as additional feed for poultry and other animals. Really could be a win-win all round

author: @alexanderwebb680
text:
There is no vitamin A in any plants. 
Vitamin A is retinol. What you're calling Vitamin A, is probably a carotenoid, which is a *vitamin precursor*, or a molecule that *can* be converted into an actual vitamin like retinol, in the gut of a ruminant animal like a cow or sheep. 
Calling a carotenoid, Vitamin A, is precisely as false as calling Cholesterol, Testosterone.

author: @craigsurette3438
text:
You are hearing bullcrap about how hard it is to make acorns edible.

My Native trained Ethnobotany of New England professor, fed us acorn meal regularly, as it was a staple food here in the US Northeast. She made hers by hulling and decapping the acorns, breaking them up coarsely, and then putting them in a cloth bag in the river and  waiting a month or so and then milling the results.They were absolutely delicious and there was only a tiny suggestive hint of the tannic bitter left.

 In an afternoon of light work, with a couple of friends and then a month of passive soaking time, you can make enough food for all of you to very robustly supplement your diet for the winter.

author: @fritzwilhelm8258
text:
John Kallas, of Wild Food Adventures here in the Pacific Northwest of America, has shown that leaching of tannins can be achieved in a few hours of an afternoon.  High pressure spray from a hose into a flat pan with a supported filter, allowed to drain several times will do the trick.  With this cold water treatment many of the fats and oils are preserved.  This is a much faster and efficient mode of tannin removal than one hears of elsewhere, as I've seen for myself when I attended his acorn processing workshop back in fall 2010.  

I've found it very helpful to use a hand-cranked nut cracking machine called the DaveBilt nutcracker.  If the nuts are first dried and sent through the mill, mostly wholes and halves can be obtained to the tune of 40-50 lbs an hour (depending on your stamina!).  Both are very effective methods that greatly reduce production time and the overall usability of native nuts and their oils.

FYI 1% of corn is used for food(U.S.). The 20% of corn exported is used to fatten up cattle for rich foreigners even though cows poop out 80% of straight corn.  Yet if you first produce ethanol then feed the cows the byproduct(DGGs) you produce more meat for less feed. So 10lbs of corn =1 lbs of meat normally and instead 3lbs DGGs fed to the cow =17% more meat. The  cow produces meat more efficiently with DGGs than straight corn. There is no struggle to digest it for the cow.
Ethanol lowers world hunger this way.
An even better way to use DGGS is to feed it to Tilapia who produce 2lbs of meat for every lb. of DGGs.

text: Very interesting, as I tried water leeching acorns a few years ago, and failed because I got discouraged by the amount of time it took. replies.author: @HostilePride
replies.text:
Even just seperating it off and reusing it without distilling seemed practical. This process seemed to strip the tannin out pretty well. You may even be able to make a simple centrifuge that would possibly help with removing all the liquid from the acorn pulp and make reusing it a lot easier.

Not that re-distilling is a bad idea, it would help if the ethanol concentration drops too low (Either from evaporation over time or addition of water from wet acorns or something).

I wonder if you could even combine the distillation process at the end of the extraction, seeing as its already heated to 60c for the process, Ethanol evaporates near this already. I wonder if doing this could be worth it, you would be left with tannin water essentially.

This process really fascinates me, it's a shame there aren't many oak trees in my part of Australia lmfao. I would be keen to try and tan some natural tannin leather with tannin I extracted myself xD

replies.text: Weeks sounds like a long time. There was a show once on Discovery I think called HillBilly Blood. Why remove the tannins if making bio-ethanol?
Just grind the acorns soak in warm water and add pectinase 1st and 24 hours later something like Angle yellow yeast or add in a couple of kilo of high diastetic ground barley and soak for a few hours at a nice steady temprature to allow the emzymes in the barly to convert all the starch into sugars. 
Then add yeast once the batch has cooled to a suitable temprature and brew it out. Then filter and run through a still. Using the iodien test before brewing will indicate if all the starches have been converted before brewing and hydromiter readings before and after will indicate when brewing is compleated.

replies.author: @aeonturnip2
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Tannins are a group of compounds, of which tannic acid is one. Teas have a mix of tannic acid and other tannins. The bigger difference is the amount of the substance you're eating - with brewed tea, it's in the order of milligrams per cup, but if you're eating acorns then it's thousands of times more than that.

replies.author: @EmperorGoodwin
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Tannic acid is found in green oak leaves, bark, twigs, and such.  It is probably also present in green acorns, but that apparently is converted as the acorn turns brown allowing the nut to prepare for sprouting.

The tannic acid in oak (as well as walnut and some other trees) wood varies by variety, white oak being higher than red.  Over time the tannins turn darker which is why very old oak has near-black streaks in the grain.  Often oak furniture is fumed with ammonia to accelerate the process.  As a wood-worker myself I've often used tea as a way to increase the tannin levels in red oak so that once fumed with ammonia it has the same appearance as white oak.

Presumably the ammonia reacting comparably with tea tannins and oak tannins suggests they're chemically very similar.

Interestingly enough other nut trees produce "toxic" chemicals as well in their leaves, twigs, bark, roots, etc. Black walnut produces high levels of juglone which will kill many plants as well as insects that feed on it.  Walnut husks were also used to chemically "stun" fish for easy harvesting.

replies.author: @Rainbow_Oracle
replies.text:
Yeah, corn in the USA generically refers only to what used to be known as 'Indian corn'. People just got lazy and stopped mentioning the Indian part, once eating "the food of the other people" stopped being a taboo, and it became a normal part of the colonial American diet. 'Maize' is the proper name of that crop. 

Though that word maize comes from the Taino Indians that the Spanish wiped off the face of the earth, the term did survive in Spanish as 'maíz' (see the resemblance?), but it never actually became popular as such in the English colonies. 

I like the French form 'maïs' best, as it is written unambiguously, and like how it's supposed to be pronounced. Considering that English prefers French forms of Latin words, I'm mildly surprised it didn't catch on in English, like how the similar word "naïve" did.

replies.author: @RealitiesCookiejar
replies.text: ​@Rainbow_Oracle it changed to 'corn' because the sacks that grains were transported in simply read 'corn' to differentiate them from sacks of wool or other materials, but since maize is a heartier plant than other grains and because its the highest yielding grain, it became the pioneer crop for the american frontier for the planting and working of newly established fields mostly as a feed for pigs or cows or chickens therefore a sack that had 'corn' written on it was almost certain to contain maize and nothing else and so the rest is history. the word 'corn' as a substitute for maize didnt really permeate outside of north america until the 1940's and when it did, it was mostly because the grains america sent around the place during that time and into the 1950's, was mostly maize either packaged as 'corn' or (and more specifically) 'corn bread'.

author: @megahumegahu5799
text:
Hello Robert, very nice new way to get rid of tannins. I am really into acorns and have studied and tried different approaches. 
I just finished my experiment with what I think is biological degradation of tannins. Many microorganisms are known to produce enzyme tannase that decompose that nasty bitter substance. I have followed the recommandation of one guy from Spain and it really worked.
I have used a Red Oak acorns that are really packed with tannins (even wild annimals eat the other ones, if possible). Then I made a simple 6% salt solution and submerged acorns into it. After 10 month, I have tasted them and they were completely bitter-free.
My theory is, that some microorganisms that can survive this brine produced tannase and completely decomposed tannins. Definetely needs more research, but it looks like safe and energy free (but long) approach. Brine also serve as preservative, so it doesn't spoil... 😉
Maybe that burying them in the bog soil, as North American indians did, works the same way (anaerobic bacteria producing tannase).
